name: Dill Pickles
recipeIngredient: PLUS one or more of the following:
recipeInstructions:
Use the quantities given above for each gallon capacity of your container.
Procedure: Wash cucumbers. Cut a 1/16-inch slice off the blossom end and discard. Leave 1/4-inch of stem attached. Place half of dill and spices on bottom of a clean, suitable container. Add cucumbers, remaining dill, and spices. Dissolve salt in vinegar and water mixture and pour over cucumbers. Add suitable cover and weight. Store at temperatures between 70°F and 75°F for about 3 to 4 weeks while fermenting. Temperatures of 55°F to 65°F are acceptable, but fermentation will take 5 to 6 weeks. Avoid temperatures above 80°F or pickles will become too soft during fermentation. Fermenting pickles cures slowly. Check the container several times a week and promptly remove surface scum or mold. If the pickles become soft, slimy, or develop an unpleasant odor, discard them. Fully fermented pickles may be stored in the original container for about 4 to 6 months, provided they are refrigerated and surface scum and molds are removed regularly. Canning fully fermented pickles is recommended. Pour brine into a pan, heat slowly to a boil, and simmer 5 minutes. Filter through paper coffee filters if desired. Fill jar with pickles and hot brine, leaving 1/2-inch headspace. Adjust lids and process as recommended in Table 1 or use the low-temperature pasteurization treatment described below.
The following treatment results in better product texture but must be carefully managed to avoid spoilage. Place jars in a canner filled halfway with warm (120°F to 140°F) water. Add hot water to cover jars by at least 1 inch. Heat until water temperature reaches and maintains 180°F to 185°F for 30 minutes. Check with a candy or jelly thermometer to ensure the water remains above 180°F during the entire period. Temperatures higher than 185°F may cause unnecessary softening of pickles.
